data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Returns
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 2.5 percentage points to 36.8%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in four weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 7.4 percentage points to 29.2%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 53rd time in 55 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 4.9 percentage points to 34.0%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 34th time in 36 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 2.5 percentage points to 2.7%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 24th time in 25 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they think tariffs will impact economic growth in the second half of 2025.

Here is how they responded:

  • They will slow the U.S. economy by a significant amount: 22.8%.
  • They will slow the U.S. economy by a modest amount: 45.9%.
  • They will have little/no impact on the U.S. economy: 21.0%.
  • They will boost the U.S. economy: 6.6%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 3.1%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 36.8%, down 2.5 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 29.2%, up 7.4 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 34.0%, down 4.9 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Limited Upside In Under Armour's Premium Pivot
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Under Armour, Inc.’s (NYSE:UAA) (NYSE:UA) brand has been struggling in recent years, and in 2024, the company brought founder Kevin Plank back to turn the brand around. However, Under Armour is in a downward spiral with revenue and margins down. While management believes they can turn things around with more premium branding strategies and prices, I am skeptical. I believe this turnaround will not be successful given the competitive landscape in the industry; therefore, I am giving Under Armour a Sell rating at current prices due to limited upside in a turnaround.

Under Armour Compared To Other Apparel Companies

Under Armour develops, markets, and distributes branded performance footwear, apparel, and accessories. They have major partnerships with Athletes in several sports, including Jordan Spieth for golf and Steph Curry for Basketball. In 2020, Under Armour launched the Curry Brand, which is similar to Nike’s Jordan Brand. Under Armour has struggled in recent years with revenue declines, and I believe a turnaround will be difficult. As part of their restructuring plan, management has cut their SKUs by 25% and they want to focus on becoming a premium brand while cutting back on discounts to improve profitability. Currently, Under Armour has a diverse sales mix with 67% of revenue coming from apparel, 23% from footwear, and 8% from accessories. Additionally, Under Armour has 441 company-owned and operated stores. The company also has operating leases with a present value of $704 million (4.88% discount rate), which is significant. Management believes they can compete successfully due to their brand image and recognition, although I am skeptical. Below I will present worldwide 5-year Google Trends data for Under Armour, NIKE, Inc. (NKE), lululemon athletica inc. (LULU), and The Gap, Inc. (GAP).

Under Armour’s search data has been relatively flat, Nike's has been declining in recent years, Lululemon's has been accelerating, and Gap's has seen slight growth. This corresponds with revenue for Under Armour declining by 9.8% over the past 3 years, Nike declining by 1%, Lululemon appreciating by 69%, and Gap declining by 9.5%. Finally, Under Armour had a gross profit margin of 47.9% (3yr average of 46.3%), Nike’s was 42.7% (3yr average of 43.7%), Lululemon’s was 59.2% (3yr average of 57.6%), and Gap’s was 41.3% (3yr average of 38.1%). Although not all encompassing, these numbers show the pricing power of each brand. Under Armour has a strong gross profit margin relative to peers, but its brand and revenues are declining, signaling consumers are not responding well to pricing changes. Meanwhile, Brands like Lululemon are thriving. With search volumes flat, it will be hard for Under Armour to gain brand relevancy, and it will require more spending, which I believe will make their turnaround harder. In short, although Under Armour has respectable gross margins, its brand’s relevance is flat, with revenue plummeting, making a turnaround harder and more expensive.

Can Under Armour Return To Growth

Under Armour operates in North America, EMEA, Asia-Pacific, and Latin America. Their revenue by region is shown below.

With the exception of EMEA, all regions have shown significant declines, most notably the 11% decline in their primary North American market. It is also worth noting that this comparison ended March 31, 2025, so this is before Trump’s April 2nd Liberation Day tariffs. Even without those headwinds factored in, the brand is in deep trouble.

In the next few years, revenue is expected to continue to decline, with analysts estimating revenue for next year at, $5.06 billion ($5.16 billion LTM), then recovering to $5.23 billion in FY 2027. While analysts project a 1.3% revenue growth over the next two years, I believe this will still be challenging for Under Armour, especially with higher prices (and presumably lower volume). Historically, Under Armour has not been a luxury brand, needing constant discounts to move product, and we have already started to see the effects of the higher pricing strategy, crushing revenue. Despite marketing, Under Armour’s products are still commodities, and I do not believe the logo and brand alone move product. In a time when consumers are not responding well to Under Armour’s product, the last thing management should be doing is selling pricey items. In my opinion, this strategy, without significant capital invested, will destroy the brand, making it decline further. Unlike brands like Crocs, Inc. (CROX) or Lululemon, Under Armour does not have a recognizable product or line of products (except the Curry). For a brand to have pricing power, it needs to be different. Crocs has a distinctive look that consumers recognize and pay a premium for (59.2% gross margin), and Lululemon’s brand has driven a lifestyle and community-driven identity that commands strong pricing power. Both of these brands created niches for themselves. In contrast, Under Armour lacks these characteristics; its products are functional but largely interchangeable. Without a truly differentiated product or aspirational identity, Under Armour’s efforts will destroy the brand, especially in a time when consumers are watching their discretionary spending.

DCF Valuation For Under Armour

Although many uncertainties lie for Under Armour, I will create a DCF model for the company should management be successful or semi-successful in turning around its brand. In a turnaround like Under Armour, growth would come from margins, not revenue, so I will use analyst projections for the next two years and then use a 1.75% growth rate from 2028 and beyond. Since 2018, Under Armour has spent about 3% of revenue on CAPEX, but I project this will be slightly higher (3.2%) in the future due to higher branding investment. Although Under Armour has significant operating leases, I kept them as operating expenses and only used the net debt from their most recent balance sheet, although these operating leases are significant and could be capitalized. Finally, I am using a 7.87% cost of capital with a 6.75% cost of debt (below last offering) and an 8.6% cost of equity with a 4.4% risk-free rate (10-year US Treasury), a 0.84 unlevered industry beta (1 levered), and a 4.2% equity risk premium. My full model is shown below.

After a semi-successful turnaround, I believe Under Armour’s intrinsic value is $7.73, representing an 8% undervaluation at current prices. As with any major brand shift, it is difficult to forecast how margins will look. While I used an 8% margin (after a 4-year turnaround period), other brands like Lululemon and Nike have a 20.8% and 10.3% margin, respectively, since 2018. I do not believe the most successful turnaround will give Under Armour margins like Lululemon, but it could approach Nike’s. I showed other margins in my table. My most optimistic estimate is 9.5%, which is higher than Under Armour’s historical average and comparable to Nike's. If Under Armour achieves this margin in four years, it would be worth approximately $9.50 per share. All that being said, I do not believe we will see a successful turnaround and I do not believe the upside if Under Armour is successful would warrant the risk investors would take. Under Armour’s business has not been great for many years, and there are much better places for my money given the risk of this turnaround.

Risks And Final Thoughts

Under Armour’s industry is full of competition. Under Armour’s product is not significantly different from other players, and I do not think they have the brand strength like Nike. Additionally, revenue dropping over 9% makes it a hard turnaround. Management is focusing on becoming more premium with fewer discounts, but I do not see how consumers will pay higher prices for products that are not very differentiated. The business has not been doing great since 2016, when its growth plateaued, and I see more downward pressure in the future with this new premium strategy as well as the current macro environment.

The main risk is that the company will get pricing power and margins will rival Nike or Lululemon, or revenue grows more than I have projected, although given everything I discussed, I do not see this outcome as likely.

The risk-reward is not attractive for Under Armour investors; therefore, I am giving Under Armour a Sell rating.

data.title: Alphabet Q2 Quick Take: It's Firing On All Cylinders As Thematic Discount Fades
data.description:
With a robust moat in digital advertising, alongside leadership in AI, quantum, and autonomous mobility developments, Alphabet Inc., aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) remains an underappreciated thematic juggernaut with tangible fundamental support. Yet the stock has continued to underperform its big tech peers in the broader market this year. Despite having gained more than 30% in value since the post-Liberation Day rally, Google stock is barely in the green year-to-date, while the S&P 500 (SP500) and tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (NDX) have soared 8.5% and 10.6% over the same period, respectively.

Despite ongoing investor concern about risks of market share erosion in its core advertising business since the advent of generative AI in late 2022, Google's continued to preserve its leadership. This is corroborated by another period of robust double-digit growth across both Search and YouTube ads in Q2, complemented by accelerating subscription gains, alongside sustained margin expansion for the segment. Meanwhile, Google Cloud has also continued to gain traction, with stronger-than-expected acceleration in Q2 indicating sustained share gains against industry leaders like Microsoft's Azure (MSFT) and Amazon Web Services (AMZN).

Taken together, we believe Google's competitive moat remains intact, preserving its trajectory of sustained value accretion from current levels. And while macro uncertainties loom—especially given the kick-off of reciprocal tariffs in August—Google remains well-supported by resilient fundamentals across its diversified portfolio of high growth levers.

Core Ads Fortress Still Intact

Google's continued to deliver robust results in Q2 for its core Search and YouTube advertising businesses. Despite it being a seasonally weak quarter, Google's core advertising revenues maintained sequential acceleration with 10% y/y growth. The results continue to reinforce Google's moat in digital advertising, as well as its capability in preserving growth amid concerns of AI-driven competition and looming macro headwinds.

Considering the impending tariff uncertainties, which risk triggering a pullback in ad budgets, advertisers are likely to prefer formats with proven effectiveness to ensure optimized conversions and return on ad spend ("ROAS"). In addition to Google Search, YouTube's continued to emerge as a preferred format amongst advertisers.

Specifically, YouTube's emerged as the bright spot once again this quarter. Related ad revenues grew 13% y/y during Q2, which represents both annual and sequential acceleration despite a seasonally weaker period. The sustainability of said robust results is complemented by continued growth in subscription adds during the quarter as well, especially for YouTube TV. This accordingly implies strong engagement acquisition and retention on the platform—even for its paid content—which reinforces YouTube's overall appeal to advertisers. Nielsen's also reported continued YouTube share gains in TV viewership hours, as streaming dominates the metric over traditional broadcast and cable for the first time. Specifically, streaming accounted for 44.8% of total TV viewership in the U.S. in May, with YouTube in the lead with a 12.5% share.

In addition to robust engagement and viewership reach, Google's also doubled down on improving its slate of AI innovations to ensure further optimization of ad spend. And the recent introduction of its AI-driven image-to-video feature in the Gemini app for Google AI Ultra and Pro continues to highlight the company's efforts on this front. Although the tool currently targets Gemini app users, it underpins related improvements to Google's slate of AI-enhanced ad tools as well. This would be key to unlocking further ad campaigning and deployment spend efficiencies while also enabling more content creation with advertisers' existing inventory. Not only will this further streamline ad production to deployment workflows, but it'll also optimize ROAS, further attracting advertisers to Google's ad formats.

Taken together, the setup continues to preserve Google's ad most against risks of AI-driven competition—particularly in queries with commercial intent—as well as tightening ad budgets ahead of tariff-driven uncertainties. Specifically, Google Search and YouTube have historically proven more resilience in an extended downturn. This was evidenced in 2022 to 2023—despite significant growth deceleration, Google Search and YouTube ads still delivered meaningfully better results than its smaller peers like Snap (SNAP) and Pinterest (PINS). This is consistent with advertisers' typical preference for deploying ad budgets to platforms and formats with proven success in delivering optimized conversion and ROAS metrics.

Coupled with robust engagement reinforced through the incorporation of live events on YouTube TV and a strong revenue sharing initiative with creators to sustain the content flywheel, Google remains well-positioned in preserving its leadership against emerging AI-native competitors and near-term cyclical headwinds.

Thematic Juggernaut

Cloud and AI

Beyond the core advertising business, Google Cloud has also continued to gain share. The segment grew a whopping 32% y/y, which accelerated beyond the consensus average estimate of 26%, driven primarily by the Google Cloud Platform ("GCP"). Management has also increased Google's capex guidance for the year to $85 billion, up from the previous $75 billion estimate, citing requirement to install incremental cloud capacity to address pent-up compute demand that continues to outpace supply. This continues to corroborate additive monetization of emerging AI workloads as GCP further narrows its distance from the leadership gap created by Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS. In addition to compute share gains, uptake across Vertex AI—Google's flagship LLM repository and AI builder tool—alongside Gemini AI Pro and Ultra subscriptions that have also continued to accelerate, reinforcing the company's mission—critical role in leading the AI transformation.

Autonomous Mobility

In addition to AI, Waymo has also emerged as an underappreciated asset in Google's possession. Specifically, the autonomous mobility fleet has logged more than 100 million miles without a human operator since its deployment to date. Waymo's also been rapidly expanding its ability in facilitating paid driverless rides across the country this year, with its monetizable markets now counting Los Angeles, Austin, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Atlanta. It's also recently doubled the service area in Austin to 90 square miles, with 24/7 access to better compete against Tesla's (TSLA) nascent Robotaxi operations in the region.

The company has expanded coverage of its Los Angeles and San Francisco Bay Area operations by 50% as well. Further expansion to Miami and Washington, DC, next year is also underway, while testing has recently begun in New York City, the most challenging environment for autonomous vehicles due to its congested environment.

Yet despite these advancements in autonomous mobility, Google has barely benefitted from a thematic premium in the stock—unlike Tesla. Admittedly, Waymo continues to be a loss—incurring business with a capital-intensive business model due to the business' direct ownership of its fleet. However, Waymo's first-mover advantage remains intact, with the optionality to further scale deployments and unlock sustained margin expansion in the future under an asset-lite model. This includes licensing its proprietary autonomous vehicle technology to third-party OEMs and other fleet operators, while also allowing equipped vehicles to serve rides on Waymo's platform.

Quantum Computing

Another underappreciated long-term growth lever is quantum computing. This was evident in Google's December debut of Willow, its proprietary quantum chip. Not only has Willow proven to complete a standard benchmark computation that would've taken "today's fastest supercomputers 10 septillion years" in just under five minutes, but it's also meaningfully reduced quantum error as more qubits are used—a major roadblock in the technology's development.

In contrary to the binary code formula that consists of 0 or 1 in today's compute setting, a qubit can "manifest both the 1 and 0 states at the same time." The process, known as "superposition," has been key to handling and processing more information at faster speeds, which is vital in solving complex problems in critical verticals like medicine. One of the key roadblocks to date in scaling the technology, though, remains the complexity and high costs of maintaining a favorable environment for qubit performance stability. Because of superposition, qubits need to be maintained in precisely controlled environments (e.g., temperature) to ensure their performance; the more qubits deployed in the system, the more complex and costly it is.

System engineering is key when designing and fabricating quantum chips: All components of a chip, such as single and two-qubit gates, qubit reset, and readout, have to be simultaneously well engineered and integrated. If any component lags or if two components don't function well together, it drags down system performance.

Source: Google Blog.

Yet Google has made foundational leaps on this front with Willow. The chip consists of 105 qubits. Housed within Google's purpose-built "state-of-the-art fabrication facility in Santa Barbara," Willow has successfully achieved industry-leading performance in "quantum error correction and random circuit sampling" – a breakthrough in quantum development. Although Google has yet to demonstrate immediate commercial scalability for Willow, it's evident the company remains a leader in the field that shouldn't be overlooked.

Given its favorable prospects in honing a first-mover advantage in scaling mass market deployment of quantum computing, Google continues to exhibit long-term pent-up value accretion that remains underappreciated at current levels.

Fundamental Considerations

Adjusting our previous forecast for Google's actual Q2 performance and outlook, we expect the company to grow revenue by 14% y/y to $398.3 billion in 2025. We expect Google's core advertising business growth to remain resilient through potential macro shocks in the second half as ad spending environments adjust to the impact of reciprocal tariffs. Specifically, the advertising business is expected to grow 10% y/y to $291.4 billion in revenue this year, with continued growth at a five-year CAGR of at least 8%. This should be complemented by sustained acceleration in Google Cloud, as the unit continues to capture AI opportunities through the provision of accelerated computing instances, related development tools like Vertex AI, and proprietary Gemini app and AI Pro/Ultra subscriptions.

Meanwhile, the continued increase in revenue mix shift towards Google Cloud will likely scale the unit's margin expansion trajectory further. This is consistent with robust Google Cloud operating margin expansion in Q2 at 21%, up three points from 18% in Q1 and an impressive 10 points in the last 12 months. The ensuing accretion with Google Services' higher margins will be key to enabling sustained profit expansion for the company.

Price Considerations

Our base case price target for the stock is set at $200 per share (previously $190).

The base case price target is derived under the discounted cash flow approach, which considers projections in line with the fundamental analysis discussed in the earlier section. A 10.0% WACC is applied, which is consistent with Google's risk profile and capital structure. The analysis also considers an estimated perpetual growth rate of 2.5% applied to terminal cash flows, proxied by 2029E EBITDA, to determine Google's terminal value. The perpetual growth rate assumption applied is consistent with the anticipated pace of long-term economic expansion across Google's core operating regions and appropriately reflects the extent at which its steady-state cash flows will grow at.

Final Thoughts

While the threat of generative AI erosion of Google's core advertising moat remains a key overhang on the stock, the company has made meaningful progress in addressing said concerns. Based on the latest earnings update, the company's continued to maintain robust growth in Search and YouTube ads, corroborating preservation of its leadership on this front. Meanwhile, the company's also made a significant breakthrough with its proprietary Gemini models, making it a competitive offering against those from emerging rivals like OpenAI's ChatGPT. The company's progress in AI-related developments has also been key to ensuring its core digital ad business will be able to evolve accordingly to changes in consumer search dynamics over the longer term.

Taken together, we believe that Google stock remains adequately de-risked from the impending macro uncertainties facing its core advertising business. And coupled with its leadership in emerging growth themes like autonomous mobility and quantum computing, the stock is well-positioned to deliver sustained value accretion from current levels.
